refined_fr.refined_fr: Capture and represent worksite spatial features in a machine-readable model.
refined_fr.rationale: The candidate FR bundles three distinct obligations: (1) terrain analysis, (2) obstacle identification, and (3) work-zone representation. Each is a separate functional concern with different data sources, processing logic, and stakeholder consumption patterns. The refined FR is solution-neutral (avoids 'mapping/modeling' terminology which implies implementation choice) and focuses on the core problem: making worksite geometry and features available in a structured, queryable form. Splitting into atomic sub-FRs allows independent DP selection and IM packaging.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Confirm that a worksite model exists in a defined schema (e.g., point cloud, mesh, or feature database) and can be queried by downstream planning and execution functions to retrieve spatial attributes (position, geometry, classification) for an agreed set of site features.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-006.1: Acquire terrain elevation and surface geometry data from the worksite.
FR-NGHE-006.2: Identify and classify static obstacles (structures, vegetation, utilities) within the worksite boundary.
FR-NGHE-006.3: Define and represent work-zone boundaries and restricted areas in the spatial model.
FR-NGHE-006.4: Persist and provide access to the worksite spatial model for planning and execution functions.

candidate_dps: DP1: Acquire spatial feature data via multi-modal sensing (vision, range, inertial) and fuse into a unified coordinate frame; store as a structured spatial graph with feature attributes (geometry, classification, safety zone) queryable by planning/execution functions.

DP2: Decompose worksite into discrete zones; assign feature inventory to each zone via manual or semi-automated tagging; represent as a hierarchical zone-feature map with metadata (access constraints, hazard flags) indexed by zone identifier.

DP3: Establish a declarative feature schema (geometry primitives, attributes, relationships); populate via crowdsourced or operator input; validate against safety/operational policy rules; persist as a versioned, queryable knowledge base accessible to downstream planning.

DP4: Capture worksite features as a time-stamped sequence of observations; apply probabilistic filtering (e.g., occupancy grid, particle filter) to reconcile conflicting measurements; output a consensus spatial model with confidence bounds for each feature.

DP5: Integrate external reference data (site blueprints, regulatory zone maps, historical surveys) with real-time observations; use a federated data model that allows planning/execution to query either source independently or fused; maintain provenance and update timestamps.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1 + DP5 (hybrid): Acquire spatial feature data via multi-modal sensing and fuse into a unified coordinate frame, storing as a structured spatial graph with feature attributes (geometry, classification, safety zone). Augment with federated integration of external reference data (blueprints, regulatory zones, historical surveys) such that planning/execution functions can query the fused model or individual sources with full provenance and confidence metadata.

potential_sources: Tight coupling between sensor fusion algorithm and feature schema definition—if schema changes, fusion logic may require rework. Mitigation: define schema as a contract layer independent of fusion implementation.

Dependency on external reference data availability and format—if upstream data sources are unreliable or change format, integration layer becomes brittle. Mitigation: establish data adapter pattern and version control for reference data.

Coordinate frame alignment between real-time sensing and reference data—misalignment introduces systematic error. Mitigation: explicit calibration/registration step with documented uncertainty bounds.
Planning/execution functions tightly coupled to spatial graph query interface—if interface changes, all consumers break. Mitigation: define stable query API contract and version it independently.
coupling_check.notes: DP1+DP5 avoids premature commitment to a single data source (real-time vs. reference) and supports graceful degradation if one source fails. The hybrid approach decouples sensor fusion from data integration, reducing coupling. However, the coordinate frame and schema contracts must be explicitly managed to prevent hidden dependencies. Multiple unmitigated coupling risks remain; recommend design refinement before proceeding.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Multi-modal sensing (vision, range, inertial) is available and can be synchronized to a common timestamp. RISK: Sensor latency, dropout, or calibration drift may degrade fusion quality; acceptance criteria do not specify acceptable latency or accuracy bounds.

ASSUMPTION: External reference data (blueprints, regulatory zone maps) exists and is accessible in machine-readable form. RISK: Data may be outdated, incomplete, or in proprietary formats; integration cost and maintenance burden may be underestimated.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan and operational policies can be encoded as queryable constraints on the spatial model. RISK: Policies may be ambiguous, context-dependent, or evolve; encoding them statically in the model may not capture intent.

ASSUMPTION: Planning/execution functions can tolerate a spatial model with confidence bounds and partial observability. RISK: If functions require deterministic, complete information, the probabilistic model may be rejected or misused.

ASSUMPTION: Coordinate frame registration between real-time sensing and reference data can be automated or is a one-time calibration. RISK: If worksite geometry changes (e.g., temporary structures, debris), registration may drift; continuous re-calibration may be needed.

ASSUMPTION: Acceptance criteria 'agreed set of site features' is well-defined and stable. RISK: Feature taxonomy may be ambiguous or evolve; unclear ownership of feature classification may lead to disputes.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly specify: (1) acceptable latency, accuracy, and completeness bounds for the spatial model; (2) the set of site features to be captured (e.g., obstacles, hazard zones, access points, equipment) and their attributes; (3) the coordinate frame and reference datum; (4) the query interface contract that planning/execution functions will use; (5) the update frequency and versioning strategy for the model. Establish a data governance layer that decouples the spatial model schema from sensor fusion and reference data integration, allowing independent evolution. Define a validation gate that checks the generated model against safety/operational policies before release to planning/execution.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized spatial data acquisition, multi-modal sensor fusion, federated reference data integration, structured spatial graph storage, and provenance/confidence metadata management for planning and execution queries.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives raw multi-modal sensor data (LiDAR, camera, IMU, GPS) from Vehicle; streams spatial feature observations into fusion pipeline.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives planning/execution queries for spatial features and reference data; returns fused spatial graph subsets, individual source data, and provenance/confidence metadata to support task planning.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Emits safety zone violations and confidence-qualified alerts derived from fused spatial model to Safety Services.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Fetches external reference data (blueprints, regulatory zones, historical surveys) from federated sources; may push processed spatial graph summaries for remote planning.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. NGHE Solution is the natural home for spatial intelligence, sensor fusion, and reference data integration. Multi-modal sensing acquisition (DP1) and federated data fusion (DP5) are core NGHE responsibilities. Structured spatial graph storage, provenance, and confidence metadata are internal to NGHE. Minor diffusion: raw telemetry ingestion depends on Vehicle sensor interface; safety zone queries diffuse to Safety Services via SafetyAlert; planning queries routed via Tasking to external planners.

Poor fit. Vehicle owns sensor hardware and raw telemetry streaming, but does not own fusion logic, reference data integration, or spatial graph storage. Placing DP1+DP5 here would scatter fusion and reference integration across Vehicle, creating tight coupling to vehicle-specific hardware and diffusing spatial intelligence away from planning/execution layers.

Poor fit. Teleoperation is a control/interface layer for remote operation, not a data fusion or spatial intelligence layer. Placing DP1+DP5 here would conflate operator interaction with spatial reasoning, creating high diffusion to planning, safety, and vehicle control.

Poor fit. Safety Services consumes spatial data and safety zones to enforce constraints, but does not own sensor fusion or reference data integration. Placing DP1+DP5 here would make Safety Services responsible for spatial intelligence beyond its safety-specific mandate, creating diffusion to planning and vehicle control.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution module is the appropriate owner of DP1+DP5 because it is the designated spatial intelligence and planning support layer. It naturally encapsulates multi-modal sensor fusion, federated reference data integration, structured spatial graph storage, and provenance/confidence metadata. External interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) cleanly separate concerns: Vehicle provides raw sensor streams, Tasking queries the fused model, Safety Services consumes safety-relevant outputs, and Network Link fetches external references. This containment minimizes diffusion of fusion logic and spatial reasoning across the system.
containment_risks: Telemetry interface dependency: NGHE Solution depends on Vehicle to stream raw multi-modal sensor data reliably. If Vehicle telemetry is unreliable or latency-sensitive, fusion quality degrades.

Reference data freshness: Federated integration of external blueprints, regulatory zones, and historical surveys requires timely Network Link updates. Stale reference data may compromise spatial model accuracy.

Provenance and confidence metadata overhead: Tracking full provenance and confidence for every spatial feature and reference source may incur storage and query latency costs; must be managed within NGHE Solution's performance budget.
Planning/execution query interface: Tasking interface must be well-defined to avoid ad-hoc queries that diffuse spatial reasoning logic into external planners. Clear API contracts are essential.
Safety zone diffusion: Safety zone queries and alerts flow through SafetyAlert to Safety Services; if safety logic becomes entangled with spatial fusion, containment weakens.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: No revision to DP choice is recommended. DP1+DP5 is well-suited to NGHE Solution as a unified spatial intelligence module. However, to strengthen containment and reduce diffusion risk, recommend: (1) Formalize Tasking interface as a query API with strict schema for spatial graph access and provenance filtering, preventing external planners from embedding fusion logic; (2) Establish a reference data update protocol via Network Link with versioning and staleness thresholds to ensure federated data quality; (3) Separate safety zone queries into a dedicated SafetyAlert contract to prevent safety logic from leaking into spatial fusion; (4) Document provenance and confidence metadata standards within NGHE Solution to ensure consistent tracking across all sources.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP1+DP5 (multi-modal sensor fusion + federated reference data integration into a unified spatial graph with provenance/confidence metadata) directly and sufficiently addresses FR (capture and represent worksite spatial features in machine-readable model). The DP specifies both the acquisition mechanism (multi-modal sensing) and the representation scheme (structured spatial graph with attributes), enabling the acceptance criterion that 'a worksite model/map is generated and available to planning/execution functions for an agreed set of site features.' The hybrid DP is logically sound and complete.

diffusion_risks: Telemetry ingestion dependency: NGHE Solution relies on Vehicle to stream raw multi-modal sensor data (LiDAR, camera, IMU, GPS). If Vehicle telemetry interface is unreliable, latency-sensitive, or lacks standardized schema, fusion pipeline quality degrades and diffusion of sensor-specific logic into NGHE may occur.

Reference data freshness and staleness: Federated integration via Network Link introduces external dependency on blueprint, regulatory zone, and historical survey sources. Stale or inconsistent reference data can compromise spatial model accuracy; requires explicit versioning and update protocol to prevent diffusion of data quality concerns into fusion logic.

Provenance and confidence metadata overhead: Tracking full provenance chains and confidence scores for every spatial feature and reference source may incur significant storage and query latency. If not carefully managed within NGHE's performance budget, this could diffuse performance optimization logic into external consumers.

Tasking interface ad-hoc query risk: If Tasking interface lacks strict schema and API contracts, external planning/execution functions may embed spatial reasoning logic into their queries, diffusing fusion intelligence away from NGHE Solution.

Safety zone logic entanglement: SafetyAlert interface routes safety zone violations to Safety Services. If safety zone definition and evaluation logic leaks into spatial fusion (rather than remaining a clean query-response contract), containment weakens and diffusion of safety concerns into NGHE occurs.

Multi-modal sensor calibration and registration: Fusion of LiDAR, camera, IMU, and GPS data requires precise calibration and coordinate frame registration. If calibration logic is scattered across Vehicle and NGHE, or if registration parameters are not centrally managed, diffusion of fusion responsibility occurs.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution achieves 0.92 containment score and is the appropriate owner of DP1+DP5. The module naturally encapsulates sensor fusion logic, reference data integration, spatial graph storage, and metadata management. External interfaces cleanly separate concerns: Telemetry (inbound raw data), Tasking (inbound queries, outbound fused results), SafetyAlert (outbound safety-qualified alerts), and Network Link (bidirectional reference data exchange). Minor diffusion (0.08) is acceptable and unavoidable: Vehicle owns sensor hardware, Safety Services owns safety policy enforcement, and external planners own task logic. To minimize diffusion risk, recommend: (1) Formalize Telemetry interface with standardized multi-modal sensor schema and latency SLAs; (2) Establish reference data versioning and staleness thresholds via Network Link protocol; (3) Define Tasking as a strict query API (e.g., GraphQL or REST with schema validation) to prevent external logic embedding; (4) Isolate safety zone queries into a dedicated SafetyAlert contract with clear input/output semantics; (5) Centralize sensor calibration and coordinate frame registration parameters within NGHE, with version control and audit trails.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. Acceptance criterion 'worksite model/map is generated and available to planning/execution functions for an agreed set of site features' is directly verifiable via Tasking interface queries. Test cases can validate: (a) spatial graph is populated with expected feature geometry, classification, and safety zone attributes; (b) queries return correct subsets of fused data with provenance and confidence metadata; (c) external reference data (blueprints, regulatory zones) are successfully integrated and queryable; (d) safety zone violations are correctly detected and emitted via SafetyAlert. Worksite safety plan and operational policies can be encoded as constraints on spatial features (e.g., exclusion zones, access corridors) and validated against the fused model.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. NGHE Solution is unambiguously allocated as the owner of DP1+DP5 and the spatial intelligence responsibility. Interfaces are well-defined: Vehicle provides telemetry input, Tasking provides query/response, SafetyAlert provides safety output, Network Link provides reference data. Ownership boundaries are explicit, and diffusion risks are identified and mitigatable. No ambiguity exists regarding which module is responsible for sensor fusion, reference data integration, spatial graph storage, or provenance tracking. Planning/execution functions (external to NGHE) are clearly consumers of the fused model, not owners of fusion logic.

recommended_revisions: Formalize Telemetry interface contract: Define standardized schema for multi-modal sensor data (LiDAR point clouds, camera frames, IMU readings, GPS coordinates) with timestamp synchronization, coordinate frame metadata, and sensor calibration parameters. Establish latency and reliability SLAs to ensure fusion pipeline receives timely, high-quality input.

Establish reference data versioning and update protocol: Define Network Link protocol for fetching and updating external blueprints, regulatory zones, and historical surveys. Include version identifiers, staleness thresholds, and conflict resolution rules to prevent stale data from corrupting the spatial model.

Implement Tasking as a strict query API: Design Tasking interface as a formal query language (e.g., GraphQL, REST with OpenAPI schema) with strict input/output contracts. Prevent external planners from embedding fusion logic by limiting queries to well-defined spatial graph traversals, feature filtering, and metadata retrieval.

Isolate SafetyAlert contract: Define SafetyAlert as a dedicated output interface for safety zone violations and confidence-qualified alerts. Prevent safety zone definition logic from leaking into spatial fusion by treating safety zones as queryable attributes of the spatial graph, not as fusion-embedded constraints.

Centralize sensor calibration and coordinate frame registration: Maintain all multi-modal sensor calibration parameters, coordinate frame transformations, and registration matrices within NGHE Solution. Version-control these parameters and provide audit trails for traceability.

Document provenance and confidence metadata standards: Define a formal schema for provenance tracking (data source, acquisition timestamp, processing steps, reference data versions) and confidence scoring (sensor uncertainty, fusion algorithm confidence, reference data reliability). Ensure consistent application across all spatial features and sources.

Define performance and scalability constraints: Specify acceptable latency for spatial graph queries, storage limits for provenance metadata, and throughput requirements for multi-modal sensor fusion. Ensure NGHE Solution's performance budget accommodates these constraints without diffusing optimization logic to external consumers.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence. The DP (multi-modal sensor fusion + federated reference data integration) is logically sound and the NGHE Solution module is the appropriate owner with 0.92 containment. However, the gate results identify six material diffusion risks and seven recommended revisions that are architectural and operational in nature. These risks—particularly telemetry interface standardization, reference data staleness, provenance overhead, tasking API strictness, safety zone logic entanglement, and sensor calibration scattering—are not resolved by the current FR-DP-IM specification. They require decomposition into sub-level FRs that explicitly address interface contracts, data quality governance, and calibration management. Given high safety criticality, deriving sub-FRs to formalize these concerns is prudent before proceeding to detailed design. This is a controlled zigzag: the parent FR is sound, but its realization depends on sub-level functional requirements that govern how DP1+DP5 are integrated and operated.
next_level_sub_frs: FR1.1: Standardize and validate multi-modal sensor telemetry input (LiDAR, camera, IMU, GPS) with synchronized timestamps, coordinate frame metadata, and calibration parameters, meeting latency and reliability SLAs required by fusion pipeline.

FR1.2: Manage external reference data (blueprints, regulatory zones, historical surveys) via versioned, federated integration with staleness thresholds, conflict resolution, and update protocols to ensure spatial model accuracy.
FR1.3: Implement provenance and confidence metadata tracking for all spatial features and reference sources, with formal schema and performance-bounded storage/query latency.
FR1.4: Provide spatial intelligence query interface (Tasking) as a strict, schema-validated API (e.g., GraphQL or REST/OpenAPI) that prevents external planners from embedding fusion logic.
FR1.5: Isolate safety zone definition and evaluation as a queryable spatial graph attribute contract (SafetyAlert interface) to prevent safety logic diffusion into fusion core.
FR1.6: Centralize and version-control multi-modal sensor calibration parameters and coordinate frame registration matrices with audit trails and traceability.

revise.why: The current DP (DP1+DP5) specifies the logical solution (sensor fusion + reference data integration into spatial graph) but does not explicitly address the operational and architectural mechanisms required to mitigate diffusion risks. Revise DP to include: (a) Telemetry interface contract with standardized schema and SLAs; (b) Reference data versioning and staleness protocol; (c) Provenance and confidence metadata standards; (d) Tasking query API design constraints; (e) SafetyAlert isolation contract; (f) Sensor calibration centralization and version control. These are design decisions that belong in the DP layer and should be made explicit before sub-FR decomposition proceeds.

assumptions: NGHE Solution has sufficient computational and storage capacity to support multi-modal sensor fusion, reference data integration, and provenance tracking without performance degradation.
Vehicle telemetry interface can be standardized and formalized with agreed-upon schema and SLAs.
External reference data sources (blueprints, regulatory zones, surveys) are accessible via Network Link and can be versioned and managed.
Planning and execution functions external to NGHE can be constrained to use a formal query API (Tasking) without embedding fusion logic.
Safety Services can enforce safety zone policies via SafetyAlert interface without requiring fusion logic to embed safety constraints.
Sensor calibration and coordinate frame registration can be centrally managed within NGHE with version control and audit trails.
open_risks: Telemetry ingestion dependency: Vehicle may not reliably stream multi-modal sensor data with standardized schema and latency guarantees. Mitigation: Formalize Telemetry interface contract with explicit SLAs and fallback mechanisms.

Reference data staleness and inconsistency: External sources may provide stale or conflicting data, compromising spatial model accuracy. Mitigation: Establish versioning, staleness thresholds, and conflict resolution rules via Network Link protocol.

Provenance and confidence metadata overhead: Tracking full provenance chains may incur unacceptable storage and query latency. Mitigation: Define performance budget and implement selective provenance tracking (e.g., sampling, aggregation).

Tasking interface ad-hoc query risk: External planners may embed spatial reasoning logic into queries, diffusing fusion intelligence. Mitigation: Enforce strict query API schema (GraphQL/OpenAPI) with validation and rate limiting.

Safety zone logic entanglement: Safety zone definition and evaluation may leak into spatial fusion, weakening containment. Mitigation: Isolate safety zones as queryable spatial graph attributes with dedicated SafetyAlert contract.

Multi-modal sensor calibration scattering: Calibration logic may be scattered across Vehicle and NGHE, causing diffusion of fusion responsibility. Mitigation: Centralize calibration parameters in NGHE with version control and audit trails.

High safety criticality: Spatial model errors (e.g., incorrect safety zone boundaries, stale reference data) could lead to safety incidents. Mitigation: Implement validation, testing, and monitoring to ensure spatial model accuracy and safety compliance.
evidence_needed: Formal specification of Telemetry interface contract (schema, timestamp synchronization, coordinate frame metadata, calibration parameters, latency/reliability SLAs).
Reference data versioning and update protocol specification (version identifiers, staleness thresholds, conflict resolution rules, update frequency).
Provenance and confidence metadata schema (data source, acquisition timestamp, processing steps, reference data versions, confidence scoring methodology).
Tasking query API specification (GraphQL schema or OpenAPI definition, query constraints, rate limiting, validation rules).
SafetyAlert interface contract (safety zone definition, evaluation logic, alert output format, confidence thresholds).
Sensor calibration and coordinate frame registration specification (calibration parameters, registration matrices, version control, audit trail format).
Performance and scalability constraints (acceptable query latency, storage limits for provenance, throughput requirements for sensor fusion).
Test cases and acceptance criteria for each sub-FR (e.g., telemetry schema validation, reference data staleness detection, provenance tracking accuracy, query API strictness).
Safety analysis and hazard mitigation plan addressing spatial model errors and their impact on worksite safety.
